---
title: "Threat Models of the Doom Debate"
subtitle: "Mapping AI Risk Viewpoints, Tree-Organized (v2)"
date: "March 2026"
author: "[Author TBD]"
---

::: {.callout-note .meta-note collapse="true" title="Document Meta — v2 experiment"}
This is an **alternative organization** of the Threat Models post. The content is the same six worldviews, but §III is regrouped by **position on the shared tree** (Posts 1–2) instead of presented as a flat list. Worldviews that share a mechanism are nested under one node and differentiated on the other axes; one tree branch turns out to have no champion, and a couple of positions are handled as *distributional* claims about the tree rather than locations on it.

This version exists to compare for rigor and readability against the flat-list (v1) version. Keep both until we decide. v2 turns on one idea, stated in the intro and operationalized in §II: empirical questions that evidence can settle versus the axes you argue along that it can't. The gallery (§III) places people on the tree; the cruxes (§IV) are the bets that move them.
:::

---

## I. Why This Map Exists

::: {.aside .meta-note}
**META:** States the thesis (two kinds of question) as the hook and hands off. Withholds the diagnostic and the named axes for §II so the two sections don't double-explain.
:::

The AI debate is confused, and people talk past each other. The same p(doom) can hide opposite pictures of the world; two very different numbers can hide the same one. A decade of shared evidence has barely moved anyone. When evidence stops moving a disagreement, the disagreement was never about evidence.

Every estimate tangles two kinds of question. Some are empirical, and evidence settles them: does power-seeking come with capability, would a failure show before it was terminal. Some are not empirical at all: what counts as doom, whether an AI that quietly replaces us is a catastrophe or a worthy heir, over what horizon we are even asking, whether a calm model is reassurance or camouflage. No experiment touches the second kind; they decide what the experiments mean. Confuse the two and you argue forever.

The first two posts built the tree. This one puts people on it, placing the names that keep coming up, Yudkowsky, Bengio, LeCun, Christiano, Critch, where their reasoning actually sits. Read that way, the loudest fights get smaller and stranger: allies who agree on nothing but the conclusion, opponents a single crux apart.

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/framing.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/intro.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/a-knowing-optimizer.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/b-distributed.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/c-the-mistake.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/d-unwitting.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/e-distributional.qmd >}}

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/adjacencies.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/cuts.qmd >}}

---

{{< include ../chapters/threat-models-v2/conclusion.qmd >}}
